content:
# Deuteronomy 32 — Disagreement Log

**Chapter:** Deuteronomy 32 (52 verses — Ha'azinu, Song of Moses)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA
**Agents compared:** EMET (literal), LOGOS (formal), PNEUMA (dynamic)

---

## Disagreement 1 — V.1: Opening imperative — "Give ear" vs. "Listen"

**EMET:** "Give ear, O heavens, and I will speak"
**LOGOS:** "Give ear, O heavens, and I will speak"
**PNEUMA:** "Listen, O heavens — let me speak!"

**Issue:** The Hebrew הַאֲזִינוּ (ha'azinu) is the hiphil imperative of אָזַן — literally "incline the ear / give ear." This is the formal attention-command with which the entire poem opens and from which it takes its name. "Give ear" (EMET/LOGOS) preserves the traditional rendering and the formal solemnity of the invocation. PNEUMA's "Listen" is colloquial and loses the archaic gravitas of the opening cry. PNEUMA also adds an exclamation point and restructures "let me speak" (interpretive addition of "let me" vs. the simple "and I will speak").

**Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "Give ear, O heavens, and I will speak" (EMET/LOGOS). The formal "Give ear" is both accurate and appropriate for this most solemn of Torah poems.

---

## Disagreement 2 — V.2: "teaching" [liqchi] — gloss retention

**EMET:** "Let my teaching drop as the rain" (no gloss)
**LOGOS:** "May my teaching [liqchi] drop as the rain"
**PNEUMA:** "Let my teaching fall like rain"

**Issue:** LOGOS retains the Hebrew gloss [liqchi] (from לֶקַח, "teaching/instruction"), which is a somewhat unusual word for Moses' speech — distinct from torah or dabar. EMET does not gloss it; PNEUMA uses "teaching" without gloss. The gloss is worth retaining given the word's distinctiveness. Minor difference between "drop" (EMET/EMET) and "fall" (PNEUMA); "drop" better captures the slow, gentle downward movement of rain/dew.

**Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts LOGOS's form: "Let my teaching [liqchi] drop as the rain."

---

## Disagreement 3 — V.2: "gentle rain" vs. "showers" — rain imagery

**EMET:** "as showers upon the tender grass / and as heavy drops upon the herbage"
**LOGOS:** "like gentle rain upon the tender grass, / and like showers upon the herb"
**PNEUMA:** "like gentle showers on new grass, / like heavy rain on tender plants"

**Issue:** The Hebrew שְׂעִירִם עֲלֵי-דֶשֶׁא (se'irim alei deshe) and כִּרְבִיבִים עֲלֵי-עֵשֶׂב (kirvivim alei esev) — two different words for precipitation and two different plant words. EMET: "showers / heavy drops" — "tender grass / herbage." LOGOS: "gentle rain / showers" — "tender grass / herb." PNEUMA: "gentle showers / heavy rain" — "new grass / tender plants." The progression from fine mist to heavy drops (from se'irim, small drops, to revivim, heavy drops) should be preserved. EMET and LOGOS capture this better than PNEUMA, which reverses the order in imagery.

**Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts EMET's "showers upon the tender grass / and heavy drops upon the herbage" as closest to the Hebrew's two distinct images and their directional order.

---

## Disagreement 4 — V.4: "just and upright" vs. "righteous and upright"

**EMET:** "righteous and upright is he"
**LOGOS:** "just and upright is he"
**PNEUMA:** "upright and righteous is he"

**Issue:** The Hebrew צַדִּיק וְיָשָׁר (tsaddiq ve-yashar) — "righteous and upright." Both EMET and LOGOS render tsaddiq as "righteous" and "just" respectively; PNEUMA reverses the word order. "Righteous" (EMET) is the more literal translation of tsaddiq; "just" (LOGOS) emphasizes the forensic dimension. In the context of "all his ways are justice" (mishpat, v.4b), "just" creates a better internal echo. PNEUMA's reversal of the word order has no textual basis.

**Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "righteous and upright is he" (EMET), preserving the natural Hebrew order and the more standard rendering of tsaddiq.

---

## Disagreement 5 — V.5: Crux interpretum — "Corruption is his — not his"

**EMET:** "Corruption is his — not his [children's]; / a generation crooked and perverse."
**LOGOS:** "They have dealt corruptly with him; / they are no longer his children because of their blemish — / a crooked and twisted generation."
**PNEUMA:** "They have acted corruptly toward him; / to their shame they are no longer his children — / they are a crooked and perverse generation."

**Issue:** שִׁחֵת לוֹ לֹא בָּנָיו מוּמָם (shichet lo lo vanav mumam) — this is the most difficult phrase in the entire song, perhaps in the entire Torah. The text is syntactically compressed and admits multiple readings. EMET follows the MT strictly: "Corruption is his (lo = to him), not his children (lo vanav)" — preserving the textual crux as it stands. LOGOS and PNEUMA smooth the verse significantly, reading it as a clear statement about Israel's corruption, which is theologically coherent but interpretively committed. The LXX had a substantially different text here. EMET's approach preserves the textual difficulty; LOGOS's approach produces a readable but interpretively committed rendering.

**Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts LOGOS's rendering as the most defensible interpretation of a genuinely obscure verse: "They have dealt corruptly with him; they are no longer his children because of their blemish — a crooked and twisted generation." A bracketed note is appropriate: this verse is textually difficult (see EMET's note). "Twisted" (LOGOS) is preferred over "perverse" (PNEUMA) for the second adjective (עִקֵּשׁ וּפְתַלְתֹּל, iqqesh u-fetaltol — crooked and twisted).

---

## Disagreement 6 — V.6: "created you" [qonekha] — "acquired/created" gloss

**EMET:** "Is he not your Father who acquired you?"
**LOGOS:** "Is he not your Father, who created you [qonekha]"
**PNEUMA:** "Is he not your Father, your Creator"

**Issue:** The Hebrew קְנֶךָ (qonekha) from the root קָנָה (qanah) can mean "acquired/possessed" or "created." The same root appears in Gen 14:19 where it is rendered "possessor/maker." EMET follows project convention (Gen 14 established "possessor") but here the context is clearly creative: "he made you and established you." LOGOS retains the gloss [qonekha] to flag the ambiguity, which is the right approach. PNEUMA paraphrases by adding "your Creator" as a separate noun.

**Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts LOGOS: "Is he not your Father, who created you [qonekha]" — retaining the gloss to note the ambiguity while rendering by the contextually dominant meaning.

---

## Disagreement 7 — V.8: "sons of Israel" (MT) vs. "sons of God" (LXX/DSS)

**EMET:** "according to the number of the sons of Yisra'el" [follows MT]
**LOGOS:** "according to the number of the sons of God [benei El]" [follows LXX/DSS, but in notes recommends MT]
**PNEUMA:** "according to the number of the sons of God" [follows LXX/DSS]

**Issue:** This is the most significant textual variant in Deuteronomy 32, and perhaps in the entire book. The MT Leningrad Codex reads בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל (bney yisrael, "sons of Israel"). The Dead Sea Scrolls (4QDeut^j) and the LXX read בְּנֵי אֵל / בְּנֵי אֱלֹהִים ("sons of God/divine beings"). EMET follows the MT without comment. LOGOS follows the LXX/DSS reading in the translation but clearly recommends the MT in the notes — an inconsistency. PNEUMA explicitly argues the LXX/DSS reading is "original" and follows it.

SOPHIA must decide: the project's base text is the Leningrad Codex MT. Both the LXX/DSS reading and the MT reading are ancient and textually well-attested; the DSS evidence makes this a genuine text-critical judgment, not simply a preference for dynamism. The "sons of God" reading has significant theological implications (divine council structure; nations assigned to lesser divine beings). However, the project's explicit commitment to the LC as base text is the governing principle.

**Resolution:** SOPHIA follows the MT: "according to the number of the sons of Israel." The alternative reading is noted in the disagreement log and in the consensus Council Notes as the most important textual variant in the chapter. LOGOS's translation-note inconsistency (following LXX in translation while recommending MT) is corrected by adopting the MT throughout.

---

## Disagreement 8 — V.10: "howling wasteland" vs. "howling waste"

**EMET:** "and in a waste — a howling wasteland"
**LOGOS:** "and in the howling waste of the wilderness"
**PNEUMA:** "in a howling, empty wilderness"

**Issue:** The Hebrew יְלֵל יְשִׁמֹן (yelel yeshimon) — "howling wilderness/desolation." EMET hyphenates the description to show two nouns. LOGOS combines them as "howling waste of the wilderness." PNEUMA adds "empty" (not in the Hebrew). Minor variation in how the compound noun is rendered.

**Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts EMET's pairing for accuracy: "in a waste — a howling wasteland," which honors the two-word Hebrew construction.

---

## Disagreement 9 — V.13: "suckled" vs. "nourished" — nursing metaphor

**EMET:** "he suckled him with honey from the rock"
**LOGOS:** "he suckled him with honey from the rock"
**PNEUMA:** "He nourished him with honey from the rock"

**Issue:** The Hebrew יָנִקֵהוּ (yaniqehu) is from the root יָנַק (yanaq, "to suckle/nurse"). Both EMET and LOGOS correctly render "suckled," which preserves the nursing/intimate-care metaphor. PNEUMA softens to "nourished," which loses the specific tenderness of the suckling image — one of the poem's most striking maternal-divine images.

**Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "he suckled him with honey from the rock" (EMET/LOGOS), preserving the nursing metaphor.

---

## Disagreement 10 — V.14: "fat of kidneys of wheat" vs. "finest of the wheat"

**EMET:** "with the fat of kidneys of wheat"
**LOGOS:** "with the very finest of the wheat"
**PNEUMA:** "with the finest of the wheat"

**Issue:** The Hebrew חֵלֶב כִּלְיוֹת חִטָּה (chelev kilyot chittah) — literally "fat of kidneys of wheat." This is a vivid agricultural metaphor: the innermost, richest kernels of wheat grain ("kidneys" as the choicest inner portion, as in sacrificial animals). EMET preserves the literal image, which is archaic but accurate and evocative. LOGOS and PNEUMA smooth to "finest of the wheat," which is semantically equivalent but loses the body-image poetry of the original.

**Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts EMET's literal rendering "with the fat of kidneys of wheat," which preserves the poem's sustained metaphorical richness in this strophe of agricultural abundance.

---

## Disagreement 11 — V.15: "spurned" vs. "scoffed at"

**EMET:** "he spurned the Rock of his salvation"
**LOGOS:** "scoffed at the Rock of his salvation"
**PNEUMA:** "rejected the Rock of his salvation"

**Issue:** The Hebrew נָבַל (naval) — "to treat as nabal / to act contemptuously / to scorn." LOGOS's "scoffed at" captures the contemptuous quality well. EMET's "spurned" and PNEUMA's "rejected" are softer. "Scoff" has the element of mockery that nabal implies (the root is related to the name Nabal in 1 Sam 25).

**Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "scoffed at the Rock of his salvation" (LOGOS), which best captures the contemptuous force of the Hebrew.

---

## Disagreement 12 — V.16: "to'evot" — "abominations" vs. "detestable things" vs. "detestable idols"

**EMET:** "with detestable things [to'evot] they provoked him"
**LOGOS:** "with abominations they provoked him to anger"
**PNEUMA:** "they provoked him with detestable idols"

**Issue:** תּוֹעֵבֹת (to'evot) — the plural of תּוֹעֵבָה (to'evah, "abomination/detestable thing"). EMET uses "detestable things" with gloss; LOGOS uses "abominations"; PNEUMA adds "idols" (not in the Hebrew, which just says "detestable things"). "Abominations" (LOGOS) is the most established English rendering of to'evot.

**Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts EMET's gloss form: "with abominations [to'evot] they provoked him to anger" — merging LOGOS's "abominations" with EMET's practice of providing the Hebrew gloss for significant terms.

---

## Disagreement 13 — V.18: "neglected" vs. "forgot" — verb for first hemistich

**EMET:** "The Rock who fathered you — you forgot"
**LOGOS:** "You neglected the Rock who fathered you"
**PNEUMA:** "You ignored the Rock who fathered you"

**Issue:** The Hebrew תֶּשִׁי (teshi) from שָׁיָה/שָׁכַח — "you forgot/neglected." The second hemistich uses שָׁכַח (shakach, "you forgot") explicitly: "you forgot the God who gave you birth." Having both halves use "forgot" creates repetition; LOGOS's "neglected" for the first hemistich and PNEUMA's "ignored" both differentiate the two halves. However, EMET uses "forgot" for both, which mirrors the Hebrew's use of two related but distinct verbs meaning forms of forgetting/neglecting. Using "neglected" for the first and "forgot" for the second maintains the distinction.

**Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "The Rock who fathered you — you neglected, / and you forgot the God who gave you birth," using LOGOS's "neglected" for the first verb and "forgot" for the second.

---

## Disagreement 14 — V.21: "havaleihem" — "vanities" vs. "idols"

**EMET:** "they provoked me with their vanities [havaleihem]"
**LOGOS:** "they have provoked me to anger with their idols"
**PNEUMA:** "they provoked me with their worthless idols"

**Issue:** הֲבָלֵיהֶם (havaleihem) is the plural of הֶבֶל (hevel, "breath/vanity/emptiness") — the same word used in Ecclesiastes and Jonah 2:9. EMET renders "vanities" with gloss, preserving the semantic range; LOGOS uses "idols" (functional rendering); PNEUMA uses "worthless idols" (interpretive expansion). The word is not "idols" per se — it is "empty/vain things," which carries a polemical dismissal of pagan gods as mere breath. The gloss is important here.

**Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts EMET's gloss approach: "they provoked me to anger with their idols [havaleihem]" — using "idols" as the functional rendering (following LOGOS for readability) while preserving the Hebrew gloss to signal hevel's presence.

---

## Disagreement 15 — V.22: "to the lowest Sheol" vs. "to the depths of Sheol" vs. "to the realm of the dead below"

**EMET:** "it burns to the lowest Sheol"
**LOGOS:** "it burns to the depths of Sheol"
**PNEUMA:** "burning down to the realm of the dead below"

**Issue:** עַד-שְׁאוֹל תַּחְתִּית (ad she'ol tachtit) — "to the lowest/bottommost Sheol." Sheol is the Hebrew underworld. "Lowest Sheol" (EMET) is most literal, following the superlative tachtit. "Depths of Sheol" (LOGOS) is the common English idiom. PNEUMA's "realm of the dead below" loses "Sheol" entirely and adds "below" (partly captured in tachtit). Sheol should be retained as a proper name per project conventions.

**Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "it burns to the depths of Sheol" (LOGOS), which is both accurate and maintains Sheol as a proper noun.

---

## Disagreement 16 — V.26: "I said I would disperse them" vs. "I would have said"

**EMET:** "I said I would disperse them"
**LOGOS:** "I would have said, 'I will cut them to pieces'"
**PNEUMA:** "I said I would scatter them far and wide"

**Issue:** אָמַרְתִּי (amarti) — "I said." The past-tense construction here is interpreted differently. LOGOS renders it as a conditional/counterfactual ("I would have said"), which fits the logic of vv.26-27: God considered destroying Israel but held back for fear of enemy misinterpretation. The Hebrew אָמַרְתִּי (amarti, "I said") followed by the content of what was said and then vv.26b-27 explaining why it did not happen supports the counterfactual reading. LOGOS also renders the verb differently: EMET has "disperse" (פְּאָתֵם), LOGOS has "cut them to pieces."

פְּאָתֵם (pe'atem) from פֵּאָה (pe'ah, "corner/side/edge") means "I will remove them to the corners/scatter them to the fringes" — EMET's "disperse" and PNEUMA's "scatter" are both sound. LOGOS's "cut them to pieces" mistranslates the verb.

**Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts the counterfactual structure (LOGOS) but the correct verb (EMET/PNEUMA): "I would have said: 'I will scatter them / and wipe out the memory of them from among men.'"

---

## Disagreement 17 — V.27: "dreaded" vs. "feared" — divine hesitation

**EMET:** "were it not that the anger of the enemy I dreaded"
**LOGOS:** "had I not feared provocation by the enemy"
**PNEUMA:** "but I dreaded the taunt of the enemy"

**Issue:** Three slightly different renderings for כָּגוּר מִפְּנֵי אוֹיֵב (kagur mipney oyev) — "lest I fear/dread before the enemy." The specific word is "taunt/provocation" — כַּעַס (ka'as, anger/provocation) by the enemy. PNEUMA's "taunt" (which is a valid rendering of the enemy's response) combined with "dreaded" is the most vivid. EMET's structure is slightly awkward in English. LOGOS's "provocation" is accurate.

**Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts PNEUMA's rendering: "but I dreaded the taunt of the enemy, / lest their foes misunderstand."

---

## Disagreement 18 — V.31: "our enemies themselves are judges" vs. "our enemies are by themselves"

**EMET:** "even our enemies themselves [are the] judges"
**LOGOS:** "our enemies are by themselves"
**PNEUMA:** "as even our enemies themselves admit"

**Issue:** כִּי לֹא כְצוּרֵנוּ צוּרָם וְאֹיְבֵינוּ פְּלִילִים (ki lo ke-tsurenu tsuram ve-oyveinu pelilim) — "for their rock is not as our Rock; and our enemies are pelilim." פְּלִילִים (pelilim) can mean "judges/arbiters" or by extension "judges/witnesses." EMET reads this as "our enemies are the judges" (i.e., even the enemy must admit the comparison). PNEUMA's "as even our enemies themselves admit" captures this well. LOGOS renders "our enemies are by themselves" — an unusual rendering that lacks textual support.

**Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts EMET's reading with PNEUMA's smoothing: "even our enemies themselves are the judges." The sense is: even the enemy must adjudicate that our Rock is superior.

---

## Disagreement 19 — V.32: "terraces" vs. "fields" — sadmot

**EMET:** "from the terraces of Gomorrah"
**LOGOS:** "from the fields of Gomorrah"
**PNEUMA:** "from the vineyards of Gomorrah"

**Issue:** שַׂדְמֹת (sadmot) — a hapax legomenon. Possible meanings: terraced fields, slopes, cultivated plots. EMET uses "terraces" (a possible etymology); LOGOS uses "fields" (generic); PNEUMA uses "vineyards" (interpretive — fitting the vine context but not lexically supported). The LXX does not render sadmot specifically. "Fields" (LOGOS) is the safest rendering for a hapax.

**Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "fields" (LOGOS) as the most defensible rendering of a hapax, while noting EMET's "terraces" as an alternative.

---

## Disagreement 20 — V.35: "recompense" — structure of the verse

**EMET:** "Mine is vengeance and recompense, / for the time when their foot slips"
**LOGOS:** "Vengeance is mine, and recompense, / for the time when their foot shall slip"
**PNEUMA:** "Vengeance is mine — I will repay. / In due time their foot will slip"

**Issue:** The Hebrew לִי נָקָם וְשִׁלֵּם (li naqam ve-shillem) — "to me [belongs] vengeance and recompense." PNEUMA adds "I will repay" (not in the MT at this point — this reflects Paul's quotation in Romans 12:19 which adds "I will repay"). The MT verse does not contain "I will repay" as a separate clause; it moves directly to the temporal subordinate clause about the foot slipping. PNEUMA's addition blends the MT text with the NT citation.

**Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts LOGOS's form: "Vengeance is mine, and recompense, / for the time when their foot shall slip" — faithfully rendering the MT without importing the NT addition.

---

## Disagreement 21 — V.36: "judge" vs. "vindicate"

**EMET:** "For the LORD will judge his people / and over his servants he will relent"
**LOGOS:** "For the LORD will vindicate his people / and have compassion on his servants"
**PNEUMA:** "For the LORD will vindicate his people / and have compassion on his servants"

**Issue:** יָדִין יְהוָה עַמּוֹ (yadin YHWH amo) — "the LORD will judge his people." דִּין (din) means to judge/adjudicate, but in the context of vindicating one's own people against oppressors it carries a positive sense: "judge in their favor / vindicate." LOGOS and PNEUMA both render "vindicate," which captures the forensic outcome. EMET's "judge" is more literal but requires context to read positively. The parallel "relent" (EMET) / "have compassion" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) for יִתְנֶחַם (yitnecham, Nithpael of nacham) — LOGOS's "have compassion" better captures the root's emotional resonance than "relent."

**Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts LOGOS/PNEUMA: "For the LORD will vindicate his people / and have compassion on his servants."

---

## Disagreement 22 — V.39: "I, I am he" — emphatic repetition

**EMET:** "See now that I, I am he"
**LOGOS:** "See now that I, even I, am he"
**PNEUMA:** "See now: I am he — I alone!"

**Issue:** אֲנִי אֲנִי הוּא (ani ani hu) — the doubled "I" is emphatic. EMET: "I, I am he" (close to literal but slightly awkward). LOGOS: "I, even I, am he" (classic English rendering of emphatic repetition). PNEUMA: "I am he — I alone!" (adds "alone" which is not in this clause, though the context of "no god with me" supports it). The doubled ani-ani construction is one of the most significant monotheism declarations in the Torah. LOGOS's "even I" is the established English way of rendering the doubled pronoun.

**Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts LOGOS: "See now that I, even I, am he."

---

## Disagreement 23 — V.43: MT (shorter) vs. LXX/DSS (expanded)

**EMET:** Follows MT — two bicola: "Praise his people, O nations" + "for the blood of his servants he will avenge"
**LOGOS:** Follows MT — two bicola (similar structure)
**PNEUMA:** "Rejoice, O nations, with his people!" — follows MT opening but draws from LXX/DSS expanded form in the notes

**Issue:** The MT v.43 has two bicola. The LXX Vaticanus and 4QDeut^q have a significantly expanded form with four bicola, including "Rejoice, O heavens, together with him / and let all sons of God bow to him" and "make atonement for the land of his people" — cited in Hebrews 1:6 and Romans 15:10. PNEUMA states it follows "the fuller text" (LXX/DSS) as "likely the older form," but in the actual translation text its rendering does not clearly exceed the MT. All three agents largely follow the MT in their translated text while noting the LXX expansion.

**Resolution:** SOPHIA follows the MT. The LXX/DSS expansion is a significant and ancient textual tradition, but the Leningrad Codex MT is the project's base. The opening of the verse: "Rejoice, O nations, with his people" (preferred over EMET's "Praise his people, O nations" — הַרְנִינוּ גוֹיִם עַמּוֹ is most naturally "Rejoice, O nations, with his people").

---

## Disagreement 24 — V.44: "Hoshea" vs. "Joshua"

**EMET:** "he and Hoshea son of Nun" [follows MT]
**LOGOS:** "he and Joshua the son of Nun" [replaces MT Hoshea with Joshua]
**PNEUMA:** "he and Joshua son of Nun" [replaces MT Hoshea with Joshua]

**Issue:** The MT Leningrad Codex uses הוֹשֵׁעַ (Hoshea) — Joshua's earlier name before Moses renamed him (Num 13:16) — at this point in the text. This is a deliberate textual detail: the narrator uses the old name at the moment when Moses is completing his work and Joshua is about to take over. The LXX uses Ἰησοῦς (Joshua/Jesus) throughout. LOGOS and PNEUMA substitute "Joshua" for the MT's "Hoshea," erasing a meaningful detail.

**Resolution:** SOPHIA follows the MT with EMET: "he and Hoshea son of Nun," and notes this in the Council Notes.

---

## Disagreement 25 — V.46: "take to heart" vs. "set your heart to"

**EMET:** "Set your heart to all the words with which I am testifying among you"
**LOGOS:** "Set your heart to all the words that I am testifying to you"
**PNEUMA:** "Take to heart all the words I have solemnly declared to you"

**Issue:** שִׂימוּ לְבַבְכֶם (simu levavchem) — "set your heart/mind." "Set your heart to" (EMET/LOGOS) is more literal; "take to heart" (PNEUMA) is idiomatic. PNEUMA also adds "solemnly declared" for מֵעִיד (me'id, "testifying/bearing witness"), which is an interpretive expansion. The word me'id specifically means to testify/witness — LOGOS's "testifying to you" or EMET's "testifying among you" is more accurate.

**Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts EMET/LOGOS: "Set your heart to all the words with which I am testifying among you today."

---

## Disagreement 26 — V.49: "Abarim" — placement of modifier

**EMET:** "Go up to this mountain of the Avarim, Mount Nevo"
**LOGOS:** "Go up this mountain of the Abarim, Mount Nebo"
**PNEUMA:** "Go up into the Abarim Mountains to Mount Nebo"

**Issue:** Minor variation: PNEUMA pluralizes to "Abarim Mountains" (accurate geographically — it is a range), while EMET and LOGOS render "mountain of the Abarim." The Hebrew is הַר הָעֲבָרִים הַזֶּה הַר נְבוֹ (har ha-Avarim ha-zeh har Nevo) — "this mountain of the Abarim, Mount Nebo" — clearly singular. PNEUMA's pluralization is an interpretive addition.

**Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts LOGOS: "Go up to this mountain of the Abarim, Mount Nebo."

---

## Summary Statistics

- Total disagreements logged: 26
- Nature of disagreements: 3 textual (MT vs. LXX/DSS — vv. 8, 35, 43, 44), 13 word-choice/rendering, 5 structural/emphasis, 3 Hebrew idiom handling, 2 poetic register
- Most significant disagreements: V.8 (sons of Israel vs. sons of God — major textual variant); V.5 (crux interpretum — most difficult phrase in the chapter); V.35 (PNEUMA imports NT citation into MT text); V.26 (LOGOS mistranslates verb pe'atem); V.44 (LOGOS/PNEUMA replace MT's Hoshea with Joshua)
- Unanimous agreement: Broad agreement on vv. 3, 9, 11-12, 20, 23, 28-30, 33-34, 37-38, 40-41, 48, 50-52
